data.title: Rates Spark: Swap Lines Imply Some Pressure
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

There are more pressures, but they are only mild

The market discount through to the end of March centred on the freezing of the Strait of Hormuz as critical. Fast-forward to this week, and equity markets impliedly think it's not that critical after all. The oil price, of course, has come under renewed upward pressure, but only to the extent that it's the correct reaction to the ongoing closure of the Strait and the threat from Iran not to negotiate until the US blockade is removed. And bond yields, too, have shown a tendency to edge higher, in tandem with a re-ratchet higher in front-end inflation expectations (albeit mild). We continue to find ourselves in an uncomfortable holding ground as we await the US and Iran to come to some semblance of agreement. And an agreement of sorts remains the most likely outcome. If not, and things turn materially worse (not our base view), everything sells off (risk assets and bonds), apart from energy prices and volatility.

Our models continue to point to a dominant rate cut outcome in the quarters ahead, and that’s not discounting front-end pricing. A worsening in the war narrative presents the biggest risk to this trade, as the higher inflation outcome would make rate cuts more difficult to deliver, at least in the immediate few months. We still think the war is in a wind-down phase, albeit uncomfortable. But even if the Iran war ended today and the Strait reopened instantaneously (still a stretch), we are left with significant price pressure that has yet to filter through. And it’s not just the wider energy price complex; it’s also the likes of fertiliser, which risk adding to food prices in subsequent months down the line.

Big picture, the US 2/10yr curve remains remarkably flat. Especially considering we are (eventually) nearing the tail end of a rate-cutting process. That aside, we identify room for short-tenor rates to fall and for long tenor rates to remain under sticky upward pressure (at least in the coming month or so). If we are correct, and there is an eventual rebuild in the rate-cut narrative in the coming month, that typically coincides with a richening of the 5yr part of the curve (can trade further through an interpolated line between the 2yr and 10yr rates). We should see some relative flattening on the 2/5yr segment, but more relative steepening on the 5/10yr (while the overall 2/10yr curve comes under steepening pressure).

The dashboard of pain has eased considerably

Swap lines could be opened all the same

In the background, most of the pressure that we identified in previous weeks has ratcheted lower (chart above). That includes the USD cross-currency basis premium, which typically would be a signal of funding pressures. Despite this, Treasury Secretary Bessent has confirmed that some Gulf and Asian centres have shown an interest in getting access to dollars through a swap line. This indicates that there is at the very least a need for dollars, and it's likely a preventative measure rather than reflecting a front-and-centre problem. It shows that access to dollars remains an ongoing thing for the global system, no doubt amplified by higher energy prices (denominated in dollars). Basically, these operate like a cross-currency swap, on a fixed exchange rate and an agreed interest rate. And the US will provide these where needed to avoid dollar funding issues.

Thursday's events and market views

From a data perspective, PMIs will be the highlight, with consensus seeing the eurozone composite index dip from 50.7 to 50.1, so just about in growth territory. The UK composite index is expected to nudge below 50, however, to 49.8. From the US, we have weekly jobless claims numbers, which are seen to move slightly higher.

In terms of supply, we have the US auction 5Y TIPS for a total of $26bn.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: Forget The 40x P/E. Focus On The 10-Year Potential (Upgrade)
data.description:
I started my coverage of Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) in 2024 (check it here); in a few months it will be an analysis that is 2 years old. But even though some time has passed, ISRG’s stock did not appreciate much in price; it kept oscillating, and with the current drop, it is now down more than 8%.

But in the meantime, Intuitive Surgical as a company improved, revenue kept growing, margins kept expanding. This is something we can observe in the Q1 that was just reported. Even with this improvement and with the stock drop, the valuation is still not compelling by itself. We are talking about a forward P/E of 40x+, it still seems “overvalued” when looked at in isolation.

However, I already think that at the current price, there is a margin of safety for Intuitive Surgical. There is a premium in the valuation, but it is a premium that the company deserves due to the dominant position and the growth prospects over the next 10 years.

ISRG’s Q1 Earnings

Both Intuitive Surgical’s top line and EPS in Q1 were a beat, and a relevant beat, with surprises of 5% in revenue and 18% in EPS.

More specifically, we are talking about revenue growth in Q1 of 23% YoY, and in my view, it was very healthy growth. The company’s procedures (from Da Vinci and Ion) grew 17%, driven by Ion, which is in a very interesting ramp-up, growing almost 40%.

And recalling a bit of the Intuitive Surgical case, most of the revenue here is recurring. In Q1, $650 million was classified as systems, $433 million as services, and the largest one was the sale of instruments and accessories, with $1.68 billion. This means that if procedures grew 17%, the hospital is not only using the installed base, which proves the value of ISRG’s system, but also, this customer will have to repurchase instruments to use in its system since it is using it more.

Another point that I liked was that the installed base - which grew 12% - grew showing that new systems are in an interesting ramp-up. Of the 431 Da Vinci installed, 232 were Da Vinci 5. In Q1 of 2025, only 147 were installed. So the base is growing with a better installation volume, and it is also growing with a better mix.

Margins also improved. The adjusted gross profit margin reached 67.8% versus 66.4% YoY, while the non-GAAP operating income margin reached something close to 39% (versus ~34% in Q1 of 2025).

Beyond that, the guidance for 2026 is as follows. A very consistent top line growth, and with expenses growing less, that is, it already implies operating leverage gains and a solid enough momentum.

In other words, in Q1 Intuitive Surgical proved that it is in a good hardware upgrade cycle. It is also in a good momentum for the usage of this hardware, since revenue keeps growing more than system sales, as well as being able to capture operating leverage.

Why The Upgrade To Buy (And Valuation)

The estimated CAGR for the surgical robot systems market is 12.4% until 2030, according to Grand View Research. This is already a favorable tailwind for Intuitive Surgical, but I believe the top-line growth can be much higher than that in this period.

Only in the U.S. are there over 6,000 hospitals. You can argue that the installed base is already large, with more than 11,000 Intuitive systems, and that some of these hospitals do not have the infrastructure to install such a system, whether due to lack of expertise, lack of money, or lack of demand. And that is not wrong; it is a valid concern.

But I believe the total addressable market is not 6,000 hospitals in the U.S. If you believe in this trend, that it really is a system that brings advantages to surgery, precision, time, and similar factors, you also believe that the total addressable market is much larger. Let’s think of a mid-sized hospital that today has 1 Da Vinci system. It trained doctors to use the system, hired services from ISRG, and created a relationship with the company and with this trend.

If it saw improvements in indicators, saw value in the product, and saw that there is demand, if the robot has very high utilization, and a full schedule, it will see value in having two Da Vinci systems. With the Ion system, if there is demand in the hospital for oncology/lung biopsy, which is Ion’s focus, it is likely that they will at least consider installing an Ion system.

In the end, this is massive compounding in the financials. Not only because the larger the installed base, the greater the recurring revenue, but because this entire relationship is long-term. Think that a hospital that already has a Da Vinci will not buy a Da Vinci 5 or an Ion system in a week; it will take months to decide, verify demand, check if its doctors will know how to use it, and train them. So think how large Intuitive Surgical’s base can be in 10 years (especially considering that the ceiling is not 1 system per hospital and the ceiling is not only U.S. hospitals; we are talking about a company with strong international potential). Maybe in 2035 the company will have more than 2 mature systems, still with a very strong moat due to technological and regulatory barriers, and still high margins due to efficiency.

Well, and even though the valuation is not exactly appealing, I think it is “good enough” for the compounding thesis here. If ISRG maintains revenue growth of ~15% and an adjusted operating income margin of 38%, we are talking about a P/OI of ~21x in 2030.

Yes, I know it seems overvalued, and it really is (in part). We are talking about operating income, adjusted, and only in 2030, and even then the multiple is above 20x. But we have to remember that the premium here is for relevant reasons. It is for the relationship, for the moat, for the recurrence, but also for the positive surprises that Intuitive Surgical can report over the next 10, 20 years. Think that with each new system developed, there is a strong expansion in the TAM. That is, in 2035, maybe the top-line growth can accelerate even more, eventually reaching $40, or $50 billion recurring. Of course this will take a long time, but it is possible over decades, and as the market cap is $160 billion, when Intuitive Surgical starts generating $10 billion in free cash flow, the shareholder yield will already be relevant.

Risks for ISRG

But I need to say that even though I agree with this premium valuation, it is still a very delicate point of the case. This is because there are risks and uncertainties, if this trend faces challenges in the coming years, whether due to lack of expertise or due to some macroeconomic/regulatory headwind, this is a big problem for ISRG. There are already more than 11,000 systems installed, and even though there is still a lot of room, if the company does not prove that these systems are valuable even for mid-sized hospitals, we may see some market saturation.

Beyond that, there is also competition. Even if today ISRG is a market leader, several companies are investing to try to compete indirectly or eventually directly with ISRG, such as Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) with Ottava. If this competition intensifies over the next 10 years, expectations for maintaining high gross margins may change (amid a loss of pricing power or a pricing war).

Looking at the valuation again, even though I think it’s “good enough,” these are clearly multiples that already price in a lot of factors. No wonder the valuation rating is a D-. We’re talking about a P/S ratio of over 13x even on a forward basis. That is, the company has to keep growing its top line while maintaining high margins for this valuation to make sense at some point.

Finally, even though ISRG’s financials look very good, I still have some concern with stock-based compensation (SBC). It is not something that makes the company bad or invalidates the case. But the company will have to keep proving that it can reduce this expense relative to revenue as it scales so that the shares outstanding chart in the long term becomes more “attractive”.

Bottom Line

In short, I believe that today ISRG’s case is already valid and can be an investment that will deliver an interesting shareholder yield in the long term, mainly through innovation and compounding. But as I mentioned, there are risks and a relevant premium valuation that needs to be considered. Therefore, I consider ISRG’s stock as a ‘cautious buy’.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Still Room To Run With Silver Near $80
data.description:
Pan American Silver Analysis

Usually, when a stock triples in price, I don't revisit it as a BUY because the valuation has simply become too unattractive for current investors. But Pan American Silver (PAAS) is a different company than the one I wrote up at $16 back in May 2023, and with silver hovering near $80/oz, I think it's worth taking another look. The quick recap: I liked the Yamana Gold acquisition at the time because it gave Pan American four producing mines at what I considered a steal. What a buy that turned out to be.

As of writing three years later, the stock trades around $56 and carries a forward P/E of roughly 12x. That's not dirt cheap, and there are certainly cheaper miners on the market today. But for the single best pure-play producer on the silver price (which I'll back up below), I think there's still value here.

A Bigger, More Diversified Company

The 2023 version of Pan American looked nothing like this one. Today it runs 10 producing mines across North and South America, spread across seven jurisdictions, with no single jurisdiction accounting for more than 25% of revenue. That's a meaningful risk reducer for a space where one bad election or one permit fight can torpedo a year of production.

In addition to the Yamana Gold acquisition, management made a shrewd move by acquiring MAG Silver in 2025 for its 44% joint venture interest in the Juanicipio mine in Mexico (one of the highest-grade, lowest-cost silver operations on the planet). This was done with silver prices at $40, or nearly half current prices, so such an acquisition today would be far more expensive. I was firmly in favor of that deal when it was announced, and I still am. Juanicipio is the kind of asset you rarely see trade hands, and Pan American now owns nearly half of it.

Adding this all together, this is no longer a one-country, silver-heavy story with a few legacy assets. It's a diversified precious metals producer that just happens to own more silver ounces in the ground than anyone else on Earth.

La Colorada: The Real Story Here

Management recently released a revised PEA on the La Colorada expansion in Mexico, and this is where the next leg of value creation comes from.

The plan calls for a new 15,000 tonne-per-day flotation plant to process resources from the La Colorada vein mine along with high-grade portions of the Skarn deposit mineral resources. Here's the deal:

  • Silver production will average over 19 million ounces annually during the peak five years

  • Mine life extends out to 2049 for the vein mine and as far as 2068 when you include the Skarn

  • All-in sustaining costs come in at negative $22.67/oz—yes, negative, thanks to the lead/zinc/copper byproduct credits

  • Using spot metal prices, the Skarn project alone carries a net present value of just over $5 billion

  • Estimated free cash flow up to $988 million annually

Add the Skarn production to what the existing vein mine will still be producing, and you're looking at close to 20 million silver-equivalent ounces annually from one mining complex. That makes La Colorada the largest top-tier silver mine in the world by size... but more importantly, the cost profile is what gets me excited.

These numbers work because management is leveraging existing infrastructure and accessing new high-grade veins. The cheapest ounce of silver you can produce is the one where your mill is already built and paid for!

2026 Guidance and Why It Matters

2026 guidance calls for a ~14% increase in attributable silver production to 25–27 million ounces, plus 700–750 koz of gold. Costs look roughly flat year-over-year at spot metal prices, and that matters a lot.

At silver near $80 and their AISC assumption sitting around $17/oz at the midpoint, every incremental ounce is dropping something like $60 straight to the margin. This is a hugely profitable company before La Colorada even comes online.

Once the Skarn project is contributing, it becomes more profitable still.

Balance Sheet is Built for This

I'm picky about balance sheets in this sector because miners have a bad habit of over-levering right before metal prices roll over. Pan American isn't doing that.

  • $1.3 billion in cash and short-term investments

  • Total debt of $852 million, but none of it matures until December 2027—that first tranche is only $283M at a 4.625% coupon

  • The remaining $500M at a 2.63% coupon doesn't come due until August 2031

  • $750M undrawn revolving credit facility sitting on top of that

Total available liquidity is north of $2 billion against a debt stack that's well-termed and cheap.

The bottom line is that management has levers to fund the La Colorada expansion, keep paying the dividend, buy back stock opportunistically, and make other strong moves, all without tapping the equity markets.

The Silver Price Angle

Look, the stock is not dirt cheap here. It's tripled. But silver bulls should own this stock, and it's worth explaining why I'd argue this over a dozen other producers.

Pan American has the most silver reserves in the world (more than Fresnillo) and is on track to produce 27 million ounces of silver annually. According to slide 24 of the corporate presentation, PAAS has the highest correlation to the silver price of any major producer (over the past year, at least). That's the whole reason to own it. If you think silver goes to $100, you want the stock that will most cleanly capture that move. If silver rolls over, yes, this stock gets hit harder than most. Leverage to silver cuts both ways. I get that.

For investors who want silver exposure (SLV) with less operational risk, a streamer like Wheaton Precious Metals offers a more conventional risk/reward. But for leverage to the metal itself, this is the name.

The Escobal Optionality Play

Here's the upside case I haven't even touched: Escobal. Pan American's mine in Guatemala has been suspended since 2017 over Indigenous consultation issues. Once upon a time this thing produced over 20 million silver ounces annually at very competitive costs and still hosts roughly 264 million silver ounces in reserves and resources, plus gold, lead, and zinc credits.

None of this is in the stock. None of it is in analyst models. If the consultation process finally wraps up and management can get this mine back online, it effectively doubles Pan American's silver production profile overnight. Fingers crossed—and in the meantime, you're not paying for it.

Risks

I wouldn't be doing my job if I didn't flag the obvious ones. Silver is volatile, and if prices crack back into the $50s or lower, the forward P/E multiple doesn't look nearly as attractive.

Mexico is home to most of the NPV in La Colorada, and Mexican mining policy has become increasingly politically complex in recent years. Execution on a 15,000 tpd flotation plant is not trivial, and PEA economics always get re-cut once you move to feasibility. Escobal is a lottery ticket; don't size the trade around it.

Bottom Line

At $56, Pan American Silver isn't the screaming bargain it was at $16. But it's a fundamentally better company today; it throws off real free cash flow at current silver prices, the balance sheet is fortress-grade, La Colorada is about to get materially more valuable, and Escobal is a free call option. If you believe in silver over the next cycle, this is one of the cleanest ways to own that thesis. I'm staying long.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Manhattan Associates' Moat Remains Wide, And Now Is A Time To Buy
data.description:
Manhattan Associates (MANH) is currently in the middle of multiple compression due to an environment of slowed growth, and this provides a rare chance for qualitative investors to scoop up one of the widest-moat, smallest-cap stocks in the game.

The Company

MANH is an American software company specializing in supply chain and commerce platforms, increasingly via cloud-based subscriptions, alongside services and support. They were founded in 1990 and became public in 1998.

Their business segments are split roughly between services (47%), cloud subscriptions (38%), and maintenance (12%), with minimal contribution from licenses and hardware. This shows a multi-year transition they've been in, away from traditional license-driven revenue toward a higher-margin, recurring SaaS model.

The larger and more complex the operation, the more MANH thrives. They are vital to their biggest customers, but what their software does isn’t even applicable to most companies that aren’t blue chip sized. They don’t give a transparent list of customers, other than sharing that no customer makes up more than 10% of revenue. Some of their biggest customers most likely include Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and NIKE (NKE).

This is a relatively straightforward story of R&D serving as the primary means of reinvestment. The majority of this R&D is categorized as "maintenance R&D." MANH is a great example of a very high-quality/wide-moat company that has a rather limited reinvestment runway. Quality investors can’t have their cake and eat it too. You can’t have a company with out-of-this-world ROIC and correspondingly high return on incremental capital as well.

They’ve never paid a dividend and have made very infrequent small acquisitions. Most years, the majority of FCF goes towards buybacks. The only issue is that the stock has traded at a premium for so long that it simply lessens the benefit of driving up EPS. I’m not faulting management for that, of course. I like that they are consistent with the share reduction strategy.

They’re one of the few software companies that actually offsets their SBC dilution plus reduces total share count every year, and they should get credit for it. I don’t expect them to change their main strategy of not paying dividends and not chasing acquisitions just for growth’s sake.

Risk

There's a general risk that was recently quite prominent of AI putting an end to all SaaS and VMS companies eventually. This is only true on the most simplified and "lowest end" type of software. Even if you’re not a "techie," you should spend a few minutes creating your own app/software using a no-code tool. Just mess around with it, and you’ll see that, while fun to use, it is rather limiting, especially as you increase complexity. MANH’s platform is designed specifically for extreme complexity. The trust factor is still hugely limiting in regard to AI disruption risk.

The most realistic risk on a fundamental level is the slow burn of their biggest peers catching up to them in terms of their core product being better for the customer. This would take a long time to really show up in financial results, and MANH's stickiness plays a huge role here.

One of the biggest risks then ends up being multiple compression risk, which will be covered in the valuation section.

Earnings

MANH isn’t exactly cyclical in the traditional sense, but their customers definitely spend a bit less during economic downturns and vice versa.

Recent geopolitical disruptions show us how fragile global supply chains remain, though I view these as cyclical pressures rather than structural changes to the company’s long-term opportunity.

I do expect current geopolitical events to affect MANH’s revenue to some extent, farther down the line, but not as an immediate result and in a more roundabout way. Rising food and energy prices for the global consumer put pressure on MANH’s customers, so we can definitely not expect an increase in revenue from any improving macro conditions. I’m not expecting revenue to beat for the next couple of quarters.

Valuation

Shares hit an all-time high in late 2024 and are currently down 56% from that point. Let's take a look at the multiples compared to peers:

I’m not a ‘price first’ investor, yet it still stings to think about paying around 10x sales for a matured software company at its slowest rate of growth. Multiples alone don't tell the full story, and in this case their competitors are often bigger and more diversified.

While the DCF is at the core of my valuation, companies that are strong qualitatively often trade at what seems like a permanent premium. Even if this is true, there is still variance from year to year. Currently the stock is reeling from continued multiple compression. The market was clearly too bullish in late 2024, and now the slowed growth is being recognised in the price.

Right now it’s not a screaming buy, but most likely a rare chance to get a lowered premium over historical averages. Quality-focused investors always face a risk of potentially overlooking higher valuation just to get exposure to such a high-quality stock. I don’t think that’s the case here, as I see the stock around fair value, but considering the historical premium, the opportunity may not last long.

Conclusion

While not a huge company, quality-first investors have known about MANH for a while. It’s usually enjoyed a qualitative premium, but multiple compression. The biggest real risk is overpaying for an extremely high-quality yet slower-growing company. This isn’t the case right now, as I see the stock roughly at fair value. It's worth taking advantage of the opportunity right now, as this is a temporary discount on a normally premium-priced stock.

data.title: NASA: This New ETF Owns SpaceX And Could Lift Off
data.description:
Traders are gravitating to space stocks once again. With the SpaceX IPO expected to launch later this year, a lot of money is heading into the sector. And understandably so. SpaceX looks like it will be one of the largest IPOs of all time.

Furthermore, SpaceX is likely to get added to major indexes shortly after its trading debut. That, in turn, should cause more passive funds to move into SpaceX stock and potentially the space industry more broadly. That makes it time to highlight a new space ETF with a SpaceX angle.

NASA: The Bullish Perspective on This New ETF

I want to be clear, from the jump, that valuations for both SpaceX and the broader space sector seem quite elevated at the moment. From a fundamental standpoint, there is significant risk across the subset of space stocks. However, from a structural perspective, I think the Tema Space Innovators ETF (NASA) has some unique attributes that the broader investment community hasn't become fully aware of yet. This explains my bullish view on NASA, at least for the next six months to a year.

The ETF launched earlier this month. Its largest holding is SpaceX, currently sitting at just over 10% of the overall ETF:

The quick pitch for why NASA would make sense is that investors can get access to a chunky SpaceX position at a reasonable price. In fact, NASA has been trading right around the fund's net asset value ("NAV") meaning that every dollar invested in NASA gets an economic interest in about 10 cents of SpaceX and 90 cents of other space stocks without any premium to NAV.

That's a sharp contrast to other trading vehicles, where premiums have blown out to astounding levels at some points in the past. For example, there is Destiny Tech100 (DXYZ), a fund that I profiled previously. This closed-end fund shot up from the single digits to as high as $100 immediately after launch, as traders picked up on the fact that it had exposure to SpaceX. Shares once again spiked in late 2024 but have slid since then:

DXYZ's NAV sits at $19.97 per share as of the last report, so that's served as a limiter on upside given that folks have to pay a big premium to get SpaceX exposure via Destiny Tech100.

Similarly, the Fundrise Innovation Fund (VCX) started trading in March and skyrocketed higher but quickly retreated:

From a 52-week high of $575 per share, VCX stock has already dropped by about 80%. As a fellow Seeking Alpha author warned, it was not a good idea to overpay for Anthropic and OpenAI. As the headline alludes, that innovation fund's AI investments drove that particular recent hype cycle. But VCX also has SpaceX as one of its top 10 holdings. And, more broadly, the collapse in VCX's share price speaks to the risk of buying a fund at a massive premium to NAV in order to get exposure to privately held company shares.

NASA, by contrast, is an ETF and as such tends to follow its underlying NAV very closely. If NASA is trading at $30, when you buy a share, you are getting $30 of actual stock value in the purchase rather than paying a chunky premium to get exposure to these highly desired private companies.

As an ETF, NASA allows for the creation or elimination of fund shares. This is the mechanism that keeps the share price near NAV. As a consequence of this, NASA is unlikely to see a massive squeeze like we saw with DXYZ or VCX. But, for traders that are confident that valuations are going to rally in the space sector ahead of the SpaceX IPO and that SpaceX is going to have a hot opening, NASA seems like an ideal way to express that conviction.

A Counterpoint: Another ETF Exists That Owns SpaceX

A counterpoint to the bullish NASA argument would be that this is not the first ETF to debut that owns SpaceX. Specifically, the ERShares Private-Public Crossover ETF (XOVR) exists, and SpaceX is its top holding, making up about 11% of the fund.

XOVR has rallied 20% over the past year, which certainly isn't bad, but it's not the sort of fireworks seen at DXYZ or VCX. Why was there no "meme" effect there?

In my opinion, Private-Public Crossover is a hard theme to sell to the public. Retail traders probably aren't going to get excited about XOVR; the concept is too hard to pitch succinctly. And, aside from SpaceX, the rest of the top five holdings aren't particularly exciting. Specifically, they are NVIDIA (NVDA), Meta (META), Affirm (AFRM), and Maplebear, aka Instacart (CART). Big tech with a splash of FinTech lending.

A mix of VC-styled investments combined with a random smattering of Nasdaq-listed stocks doesn't seem like a compelling concept to sell to the general public. It's not one that would go viral on social media. I think that explains why XOVR hasn't really captured all that much public interest.

By contrast, NASA, which invests in SpaceX and also a bunch of small pure-play space companies, is a much easier pitch. And, for what it's worth, NASA is an absolutely perfect ticker for the theme. You could hardly imagine a stronger brand or memetic association to attach to a space ETF.

NASA Could Be a Good Piece of a Long/Short Space Trade

And, for those of you that want a hedged or semi-arbitraged position, there's also the possibility of taking a long/short position here to keep SpaceX exposure while reducing the overall weighting to "space" stocks.

What am I talking about?

Take an ETF like the Procure Space ETF (UFO). I want to stop and congratulate the ETF manager on the ticker symbol, as UFO is another inspired choice.

Unfortunately, Procure isn't in the SpaceX trade. And doubly unfortunately, the top holdings list is full of stuffy satellite-related stocks from years gone by. The #3 holding in UFO is SiriusXM (SIRI), for example. When people are imagining glamorous space exposure, I doubt they are envisioning a Spotify (SPOT) competitor.

Similarly, UFO has other top holdings in legacy satellite communications companies, which are fine businesses but have little-to-no direct upside from the more exciting things that firms like SpaceX and Blue Origin are up to.

If the assumption is that SpaceX and the space sector in general are going to attract capital all year heading into the IPO, we'd expect the other most dynamic pure-play space firms to take in funds, while legacy firms like SiriusXM won't benefit to nearly the same degree. To think about it directly, every time folks buy NASA, that puts more money into NASA's top holdings and creates a feedback loop. Meanwhile, the industrial and consumer holdings in traditional space ETFs like UFO and the ARK Space & Defense Innovation ETF (ARKX) should underperform, and then the momentum funds and algos will pile onto that, rotating money out of space underperformers back into the winners.

Result? NASA gets a virtuous cycle where inflows cause its small- and mid-cap space holdings to surge, and then algos and performance-chasers buy the breakouts, pushing NASA higher and triggering more inflows from people that want to ride the trend. This sort of move exhausts at some point (perhaps once the SpaceX IPO occurs, in this specific case). But it could go on for a good while before ending.

Tema Space Innovators ETF Bottom Line

Space stocks are quite volatile right now, as is befitting of a young industry that is still ramping up. Many of the pure-play space firms aren't profitable yet. There will be serious setbacks and corrections along the way as the industry takes off.

I'm not making an argument that there is deep value to be found in NASA or any of the space ETFs right now. This is a growth industry with a wide range of potential outcomes for investors over the next five to ten years.

That said, from a structural basis, I like the setup of an ETF having SpaceX as its top position heading into the upcoming IPO. And, unlike funds like DXYZ or VCX, NASA is trading right around its NAV, meaning that people don't have to pay up unduly to get exposure to the theme. I also like that NASA's portfolio is more specifically honed in on next-generation space stocks rather than owning a wider basket of space-adjacent industrial and consumer stocks. One nitpick worth mentioning is that NASA has a 75 basis point management fee. That's perhaps justifiable given the unique SpaceX exposure, though I'd like to see the fee move toward 50 basis points over time to make this a more competitive option for long-term investors.

Space stocks have already rallied a lot over the past year or two, and there is downside risk given the higher valuations and uneven profitability metrics for many firms in the industry. On balance, however, there are enough positives to make NASA a speculative buy. It seems likely that a ton of capital is going to be directed at this sector in 2026, and NASA stands to be one of the clearest beneficiaries ahead of the SpaceX IPO.

data.title: Horizon Bancorp Continues To Benefit From Steps Taken In 2025
data.description:
This article serves as a follow-up to my previous analysis for Horizon Bancorp (HBNC) that was published in September of last year. I called HBNC a Buy at that time, noting balance sheet improvements, debt reduction, and solid earnings growth that all supported a positive outlook for the company. Since that article was published, Horizon Bancorp has produced total returns of 10.8%, outpacing the S&P 500 by about 2% and just edging out the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) by 1.4%.

HBNC released its Q1 2026 earnings after the closing bell today, and there is little in this recent report that gives me reason to downgrade the stock. The company’s operational metrics appear to be well above average, and its credit quality looks solid. Actions taken to reposition the company’s balance sheet in Q3 2025 are paying off for Horizon Bancorp, and yet its forward PE is below 9. In this update, I will discuss why I still believe HBNC is a Buy after seeing the results from the first three months of this year.

Company Overview

Horizon Bancorp is the registered bank holding company for Horizon Bank, which operates 44 branch locations in its home state of Indiana and an additional 27 in Michigan. Horizon Bank is a full-service commercial bank that offers both commercial and retail banking services as well as individual trust and wealth management services. The company also controls several smaller subsidiaries, including Horizon Investments, Horizon Properties, and Horizon Insurance Services. (HBNC should not be confused with a different Horizon Bank that operates six branch locations in the state of Texas.)

As of March 31, Horizon Bancorp controlled $6.6 billion in assets, a 2.0% increase from Q4 2025 but a 13.9% decrease from Q1 2025. The biggest reason for this sizable decrease is the company’s decision to sell off a $1.7 billion collection of investment securities in Q3 2025. Horizon also sold $190 million in auto loans at the end of September as part of a broader repositioning strategy. While the company’s decisions certainly put a noticeable dent in the company’s total assets, the long-term impacts of this action should be improved margins, lower risk, and greater return on assets over time.

The current portfolio of loans held for investment at the bank is valued at $4.9 billion. This is a marginal increase from the start of this year. Total deposits at the bank were valued at $5.4 billion as of March 31, a 2.9% increase from the end of 2025. A full account of HBNC’s loans and deposits and their changes over the last four quarters is shown below.

Horizon divides its loan mix into three broad segments—commercial, real estate, and consumer. Commercial loans take the largest share, with investor commercial real estate being the single largest type by weight. Non-owner CRE loans totaled $1.67 billion, and owner CRE amounted to about $718 million. Altogether, CRE makes up 50% of the total loan mix. Commercial and industrial loans are valued at $1.02 billion, or 20.9%. The real estate segment contains $750 million in residential mortgages. The consumer segment is also tied to real estate, as a majority of those loans are HELOCs.

Just over 21% of Horizon Bank’s customer deposits are held in non-interest-bearing accounts. The company made an intentional effort to reduce its exposure to higher-cost transactional deposits in FY 2025. The efforts paid off as the company’s cost of interest-bearing deposits in Q4 2025 was just 1.97%, 72 basis points below average for a bank of its size. At the end of Q1 2026, it was even lower at 1.90%.

HBNC has a forward yield of 3.62%. The company offers investors a quarterly payout of $0.16, an amount that has not been altered since Q3 2022. The consensus from the six analysts that cover Horizon Bancorp predicts that there will be a dividend raise later this year.

Analysis of Horizon Bancorp

In Q1 2026, Horizon Bancorp reported GAAP earnings of $26.2 million, or $0.51 per share. This was $0.03 better than expectations but $0.03 behind the company’s performance of $0.54 per share in Q1 of the previous year. However, it should be noted that the results from 2025 included a $7.0 million pretax gain on the sale of the company’s mortgage warehouse operations. Other highlights from the first three months of this year include the following:

  • Net interest income of $62.2 million was 19.1% ahead of the $52.3 million reported in Q1 2025.
  • Net interest margin stood at 4.29% for the second straight quarter, well ahead of regional banks with similar total assets and 125 basis points better than Q1 from last year.
  • Non-interest expenses rose only 0.2% from Q4 2025 to $40.7 million in Q1 2026.
  • Asset quality remained solid with minimal net charge-offs of 0.05% in the first three months of this year.
  • Return on equity of 14.99% did decline from Q4’s 15.71%, but this metric is still well above average.
  • The bank’s efficiency ratio of 55.45% was 1.71% lower than Q1 2025 and also below the last reported FDIC peer average of 58.58%.

HBNC President and CEO made the following statement as part of the company’s most recent earnings release:

Horizon’s first quarter results demonstrate the consistency of our profitability profile and the strength of Horizon’s high quality community banking model. Annualized returns on average assets again exceeded 1.60% and the net interest margin continued to be durable at 4.29%. Notably, our strategic focus on core deposit gathering yielded significant results during the quarter, delivering 11% annualized growth, led by 23% annualized growth in non-interest-bearing balances. We are encouraged by the stability and predictability we see in our financial performance, driving significant value for our shareholders, despite what has become a volatile macro-economic environment. Our 2026 outlook is unchanged, which should yield solid balance sheet growth coupled with consistent, top-tier profitability metrics.

HBNC’s capital position improved from almost every perspective in Q1. Its leverage ratio increased 29 basis points from the previous quarter to 9.84%. Its risk-based capital ratio moved up 41 basis points to 14.77%. The tangible book value of the company is now $10.52 per share, 1.9% ahead of the $10.32 mark from the end of last year.

The actions taken by the company last year may have set it up for more success down the road, but in the short term, it did depress its tangible book value. TBV per common share was $13.68 at the end of 2024. By the end of last year, it had declined over 24%. HBNC now trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.67, above the national average for all banks in the US, according to S&P Global. This is a significant change from my last article, when I reported that the company was trading at a TBV multiple of only 1.16, prior to the financial adjustments made in Q3. If there is some silver lining to report here, it would be that the bank’s TBV per share did increase 7.8% from Q3 2025 to Q1 2026. That would equate to an improvement of over 15% on an annualized basis.

Additional Risks to Consider

As I mentioned in my first article about the company, Horizon Bank has a high percentage of its loan portfolio allocated toward commercial loans, specifically commercial real estate. Owner-occupied and non-owner-occupied CRE are added together to make up 48.6% of the total loan portfolio. These loans would be increasingly susceptible to default in the face of a decline in economic activity that could be caused by higher inflation, rising unemployment, or any number of trade conflicts that create additional strain on businesses in the US. Many banks in the US made their projections for 2026 with the assumption that there would be two additional rate cuts from the Federal Reserve, but that seems much less likely today. I found it interesting that HBNC stated that its assumptions for FY 2026 included no rate cuts from the Fed.

I also mentioned in previous analysis that there was some added risk to the bank because of its limited geographic diversity. All 71 Horizon Bank branches are confined to an area in the northern half of Indiana or the state of Michigan. GDP growth in Indiana is expected to be below the national average in 2026. Employment growth in the state is expected to be quite modest before picking up in 2027. Michigan is currently dealing with an unemployment rate that is almost 1% above the national average. A healthier Indiana and Michigan would certainly provide welcome tailwinds for HBNC.

Conclusion

The picture of the future for Horizon Bancorp is certainly not perfect. A purchase of HBNC relies on faith that the steps taken in 2025 will continue to produce a stronger, more profitable company in 2026. The TBV valuation mentioned earlier is troubling, but the stock currently trades at a forward PE of just 8.6, providing some relief from that concern. Earnings expectations for the full year of $2.05 per share have experienced three upward revisions in the last 90 days. The forward yield of almost 3.8% also provides some support for shareholders if the macro environment for banks in the US becomes more challenging. I am maintaining my Buy rating for Horizon Bancorp.

data.title: DUSA: Defending The Neutral Stance Despite Recent Outperformance
data.description:
Answering the question of whether I see the potential for the Davis Select U.S. Equity ETF (DUSA) to deliver the second consecutive year of outperformance versus the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) in a row, I would say that this is not what I expect. In other words, while I anticipate a mid-to-high single-digit gain from the S&P 500 into the end of the year, with arguably quite high volatility assuming the Strait of Hormuz situation and its impact on the commodity markets, DUSA might deliver a weaker total return.

It is true that DUSA did solidly in 2025 and the first months of 2026, but I find this insufficient to abandon the neutral stance I defended multiple times over the years starting in 2023. And today, I would like to offer three reasons why I remain neutral on the name.

Reason 1: The Factor Mix of DUSA's Portfolio

To recap, as we know from the DUSA factsheet that is available on its website, the ETF

... is an actively- managed portfolio of large-cap businesses, selected using the time-tested Davis Investment Discipline. The Fund's investment objective is long-term capital growth and capital preservation.

DUSA has a high-conviction strategy, and it jettisons holdings quite rarely, which is proved by its turnover of just 8%. Moreover, as explained on page 2 of the summary prospectus,

Davis Advisors’ goal is to invest in companies for the long term (ideally, five years or longer, although this goal may not be met).

In this regard, I suppose it is reasonable to assume that exposures we see at the moment will not change much, at least until the end of the year. For context, the majority of stocks that were in the DUSA portfolio as of my previous assessment in October 2024 are still present in it, accounting for 75.5% of the net assets.

Now, as the relative stability of the ETF's exposures is clarified, here is my argument: the DUSA portfolio is light in factors that I consider critical for achieving outperformance this year, namely growth and especially growth at a reasonable price. At the same time, it is heavy in those that I expect to restrain the upside capture, including low volatility and value.

Growth and GARP Characteristics

More specifically, as of April 17, there were 26 equities in its portfolio, with the key one being Capital One Financial Corporation (COF), accounting for 7.52% of the net assets, followed by Tyson Foods, Inc. (TSN). Both have solid Quant Growth grades of A- and B, respectively (as of April 22). However, this is just the tip of the iceberg. Delving deeper, we see that the DUSA portfolio has just 37.2% allocated to stocks with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher, which is considerably lower than IVV's 50.89%. And with the weighted average forward revenue, EBITDA, and EPS growth rates factored in, the picture becomes even clearer:

And when it comes to the average EPS growth rates for each GICS sector it has exposure to (nine in total, as utilities and real estate are ignored), DUSA is again considerably behind IVV (the consumer staples sector is the only exception).

The situation is far from ideal on the GARP front too.

Metric DUSA IVV
PEG Ratio 2.376 1.259
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.441 0.579
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.573 1.131

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

We see that although the DUSA portfolio is offering a lower weighted average Price/Sales-to-Growth ratio, the EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratio and especially the PEG ratio are notably higher than IVV's.

Value and Low Volatility

At the same time, the DUSA portfolio is quite heavy in factors that I expect to underdeliver this year, namely value and low volatility, as illustrated by the adjusted weighted average earnings yield, 24-month beta, etc.

A Few Remarks on Quality

DUSA has significant exposure to the upper echelon of the market, the trillion-dollar league, with four names represented accounting for 20.4% (vs. IVV's 39.2%). At the same time, its exposure to mid-caps is quite small, with only 6.23% allocated. So its weighted average market cap is at almost $600 billion, as per my calculations.

In this regard, I am a little disappointed with its less than 80% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher, as for large-cap portfolios, I prefer to see it in the high 80s. Besides, its weighted average capital efficiency indicators are also much weaker than those of the IVV portfolio.

Reason 2: Unconvincing Past Performance

At first glance, DUSA has been doing solidly this year and in 2025, as it was not only capable of benefiting from last year's bullish narratives like the AI economy but also successfully navigating the capital rotation (its tilt towards value and low volatility helped a lot) and the Iran war.

And this prompts the question: why not expect it to continue outperforming? Here, we need to zoom out and look at the returns since its inception in January 2017.

Here, we see that its robust returns in 2025 and 2026 YTD still did not help it to deliver alpha since inception, as underperformance in 2017–2022 and 2024 was too pronounced (it beat IVV in 2023 by 7.83%, though).

Next, over February 2017–March 2026, DUSA not only delivered a negative active return of -1.85% but also considerably weaker Sharpe and Sortino ratios, as well as a downside capture ratio of over 107%. So, for now, its track record is far from stellar.

Reason 3: DUSA's High Expense Ratio

In the articles discussing ETFs, I usually remind my readers that the expense ratio is one of the primary factors to pay close attention to when searching for a fit investment vehicle, as it detracts from total returns. And here, DUSA has a meaningful disadvantage: its expense ratio of 59 bps. For context, IVV, which DUSA has underperformed since inception, has an ER of only 3 bps.

Advantages to Not Overlook

While DUSA's factor mix and performance are not supportive of a rating upgrade, I should still acknowledge that the ETF has certain advantages. More specifically, my analysis showed that its concentrated portfolio has stronger weighted average Quant and Seeking Alpha Analysts' ratings compared to IVV.

ETF Quant SA Analysts Wall St.
IVV 3.433 3.640 4.234
DUSA 3.505 3.702 4.079

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

At the same time, IVV still has a notably more appealing WA Wall Street score.

Final Thoughts

In sum, I maintain the Hold rating on DUSA. For clarity, I appreciate its high-conviction, low-turnover approach. However, I do not expect it to outperform IVV this year, owing to its small exposure to growth and GARP stocks, which will more likely be market darlings amid the de-escalation, coupled with value and low volatility characteristics that will restrain the upside capture.

data.title: SL Green Realty: Dividend Cut And Record Leasing Fuel Their Potential Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered SL Green Realty (SLG), I highlighted their developments, solid occupancy rate, and major refinancing plus asset dispositions, preparing for a potential recovery in Manhattan's real estate while coming with a risky 99.5% dividend payout ratio.

With a dividend cut freeing up significant capital as the company advances on their portfolio rebalancing, SLG remains a Buy, trading at an attractive valuation that seems to already take into account high levels of risk due to a potential Iran-driven inflationary wave and political uncertainty.

Record-Breaking Leasing, Dividend Still Cut

Despite missing the market's estimates, SLG reported their highest Q1 leasing volume in their near-three-decade history with a very solid 16.1% mark-to-market on their 51 leases signed (929,264 square feet), with the FFO falling to $0.84 per share compared to $1.40 last year, which included $0.33 per share worth of income related to the resolution of a commercial mortgage investment.

Note, however, that the company's CFO also had a great point regarding how volatile these quarterly results can be, and extrapolating them is definitely a mistake when it comes to a company like this (especially since their massive leasing plays a key role), highlighting the following during their Q1 Earnings Call:

Our quarterly -- the reason we don't provide -- one of the many reasons we don't provide quarterly guidance, and I'm a big advocate for eliminating quarterly reporting is quarters are -- can be choppy and people tend to read too much into whatever a quarterly result is. The reality of our first quarter numbers is that we were not even $0.01 off from our internal expectations, not $0.01 above, not $0.01 below. Property NOI was better than we expected as offset by SUMMIT. It was a tough weather quarter, and so SUMMIT underperformed our expectations. But net-net, right on top of what we expected. As we look out over the balance of the year, we are headed right to the midpoint of our guidance range as well.

For 2026, SLG continues to expect FFO of $4.40 to $4.70 per share, progressing on their $2.5 billion planned asset sales and still having a solid pipeline of ~900,000 square feet of space that they expect to lease, while the same-store occupancy would reach 95% by year-end. I'll also add this quote from their CEO regarding the expected leasing activity throughout 2025-2027:

I only see one way, which is we are leasing the hell out of this portfolio. And I don't see any other way to look at it. With that does come leasing capital that we will muscle through in '25, '26 and '27. But we're going to try and get this portfolio to like in its entirety, 96%, 7%, 8% leased, which it would be unprecedented in this market for 31 million square feet

Financially, based on SLG's latest report, we see that the company remains in a solid position for a REIT, advancing on their significant portfolio adjustment plans.

The company also managed to refinance, extend, and reduce the overall cost of $2 billion of their credit facility (total is $2.4 billion), extending the maturity of their revolving line of credit ($1.25 billion) to June 2031 and reducing their borrowing costs by 25 basis points to 125 b.p. over the SOFR, terms that also apply to $750 million worth of their $1.05 billion term loan component, advancing on their plans to reduce interest expenses.

SLG also announced a ~20% dividend cut to $0.6175 per share quarterly (for a ~5.75% yield today), allowing about $50 million of incremental capital for accretive uses such as DOPs (discounted pay-offs on their mortgages) or maybe even buybacks, while they expect a big shift in cash flow in H2 2027 and into 2028 on the back of capital spending going down. This is also in line with their previous estimate of reducing interest expenses, and I'm happy to see the management take these kinds of steps to ensure the business is there to enjoy a potential turnaround.

Iran Adds Extra Levels of Pressure

Something highlighted by the New York City Comptroller in their comments about NYC's preliminary budget and plans is that the biggest risk for the city and the country as a whole will come from geopolitics.

Even if the conflict itself were over overnight, it's important to understand that the inflationary effect it can have on the economy remains significant, with war premiums expected to stay for longer, pushed more and more into the future by each extra day in which the Strait of Hormuz is closed.

This is something that goes well beyond "just" higher pump prices, with ramifications spreading deep into the global economy given the region's importance in so many vital industries, potentially forcing the Fed and other central banks into adjusting their rate policy, which has been a major factor behind the weakness seen in many REITs, which can certainly be bad news for SLG given their hopes for a rebound in the industry that can at the very least be delayed.

Regarding the potential political pressure that the market continues to look out for as a result of Zohran Mamdani's win, although it remains a risk and a reason for uncertainty, I argued before how this seems greatly overstated, and we're still talking about a strong company with a leadership position in a city with significant potential and great credit ratings. The company's CEO also added on to that idea during their Q1 Earnings Call, saying the following regarding the new tax on wealthy non-residents' secondary properties and the budget:

We feel good about the city and state budget situation as well. The rating agencies did send a message to the new administration about wanting to see some efficiencies in the budget being negotiated now and the budget that will be in place at the city level by June. And I have every confidence the budget gap will be solved through revenue enhancements, expense control and support from the state. As has been reported, one piece of that sounds like it will be a new Pied-a-Terre Tax, which the governor announced yesterday with the support of the mayor and the city Council Speaker. Once you get past the notion that we need to find some revenue enhancements as part of this budget process, I give credit to the governor for taking a pragmatic and surgical approach to ensure that all New Yorkers, residents or not, are paying a fair share. This is a concept that has the support of many New Yorkers because it narrows the focus and impact to the highest earning non-New York City residents who otherwise pay no New York City income tax and benefit from New York City's exceptionally low residential real estate taxes.

Yes, there is potential to see regulatory pressure, and I mentioned before how the market likely expects significant moves like new taxes or rent freezes on some of the company's mixed-use properties (at least), but letting political beliefs aside, let's not forget the kind of power that the Mayor has, as he cannot unilaterally raise or create new taxes. Ultimately, something that can even lead to promises not being delivered on are the constraints coming from Albany's Governor and the State Legislature, which hold the "last word" alongside the Financial Control Board (if his policies unbalance NYC's budget), which can act as a significant moderator to his proposals.

And again, even if there was no extra layer of prevention here, office property taxes represented about 21% of the city's property tax revenues (and ~10% of total tax revenues) in 2023, and relying on them even more or doing something to hurt the landlords significantly would be a threat to the budget itself, and it would be hard to see anyone purposefully break one of the city's pillars at a time when the economy is in such an uncertain spot, especially since other policies require extra funds.

Meanwhile, beyond SLG's solid results and leasing activity, we see studies hinting at a potential recovery, especially for prime office real estate, with vacancy in that approaching single digits and net effective rents growing in the double digits while new supply remained constrained, similar to several other sectors of real estate across the country following a post-pandemic boom followed by weak demand and rising costs (workforce, materials, and even debt), while the company's management also mentioned that there are 0 new space deliveries anticipated for the next 3 years.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, the Iran conflict adds pressure to SLG while they are undergoing a very important pivot, with their significant portfolio moves combined with a bad environment potentially leading to them burning some value in an effort to stabilize the balance sheet and adapt to the volatile market conditions.

Meanwhile, politics and broader macro conditions can place the company in a tough position, as even though their recent refinancing came with lower borrowing costs, the SOFR can still rise on the back of an unfavorable environment, potentially pressuring SLG, their tenants, and especially their valuation.

Valuation

For SLG's DCF to Equity valuation, I'll continue to start from a FAD of $240 million in 2026 (very close to 2025's $237.24 million), taking into account their rent increases given previous leasing activity but also a headwind from the timing of asset sales and refinancing activity, which would jump to $265 million in 2027 thanks to the record leasing activity, strong occupancy, and capital recycling. This would be followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years given their aggressive leasing, solid pipeline, and strong mark-to-market spreads combined with potential macro improvements and better refinancing terms during this time, with a 3.5% CAGR until year 10 and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

To take into account the risks due to their geographic concentration, I'm going to use a discount rate of 11%, with 77.25 million diluted shares outstanding as of the end of Q1.

This gets us to an equity value of $3.36 billion, for an intrinsic value of $43.53 per share, which is close to the current $42.59 at the moment of writing this, signaling that the market is already taking into account high levels of risk and demanding a solid margin of safety.

Conclusion

SLG remains a Buy, backed by a solid business that's aggressively leasing its portfolio, standing to benefit from a potential recovery once the Iran-driven inflationary pressure passes.

Although there are risks to keep in mind given their asset concentration and near-term uncertainty, these seem to be largely priced in given the valuation.

data.title: Maran Capital Management Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners and Friends,

The return of Maran Partners Fund in the first quarter was -2.3%, net of all fees and expenses. 1 The muted quarterly return masks intra-quarter volatility. We were up nicely through February but fully round-tripped (and a bit more) in March.

I've always invested by focusing on uncovering inefficiencies in the market rather than trying to identify or ride big sweeping macroeconomic waves. Yet the ultimate backdrop against which inefficiencies may occur provides critical context. I'm not a golfer, but as they say, you've gotta play the ball as it lies (and be aware of whether inches of rain have fallen in the last few hours or if the greens are hard as rocks).

This was an eventful quarter across three vectors: technological, geopolitical, and with respect to capital flows and financial market structure.

AI is a major technological leap that is certain to have widespread and long-term implications. It is a topic that requires meaningful study and thought by all investors. It is not a fad. Just as during the Internet 1.0 era and the dot-com bubble, we'll see winners and losers along the way. There will be froth and frauds and stocks that get well ahead of themselves. Yet the technology will ultimately prevail and have wide-ranging effects.

Software stocks declined markedly last quarter on fears of AI-driven disruption. Quite frankly, some of the declines were probably warranted. Many of these stocks started out overvalued and have had poor free cash flow generation and problematic levels of stock compensation. While AI will create tremendous change, it is still an open question as to whether it represents a broad, existential threat to labor. Of particular relevance to us, the breadth and extent of the associated stock price dislocation has created a worthwhile hunting ground.

On the geopolitical front, the markets have thus far brushed off the events in the middle east and the resulting increase in oil prices. We should not be complacent regarding these developments, but we shouldn't panic, either. As usual, the proper approach is to remain level-headed and rational in the face of this type of uncertainty.

Finally, the market has recently experienced some potential foreshocks in the private credit space that may develop into larger tremors. There is a duration mismatch between the assets and liabilities of many private credit funds and business development companies (BDCs), which is a classic way to get into trouble in finance. The market has recently seen some small "runs" on these funds as demand for liquidity has outpaced its availability. Some marks are likely stale (to put it generously). But at this point, I don't see the issues in private credit rising to the level of systemic risk. Of course, I will continue to monitor developments as they pertain to our holdings and exposure.

Against this backdrop, we came into the year conservatively positioned and with no exposure to technology or software. We don't utilize leverage, and as usual, we had a moderate cash position at the fund. Therefore, we were able to use both the volatility associated with the dislocation of perceived AI losers and the general market volatility around the events in the middle east and private credit to go on offense. I have been using the framework of looking for babies thrown out with the bathwater. Might there be one or two companies which were sold off hard on the fears of AI disruption, yet which are well-positioned to prosper in the coming years or decades despite the potential implications of AI?

So far, I believe I have found at least one. This is a company that has a meaningful net cash balance sheet (almost one quarter of its market cap is on the balance sheet in cash), generates a lot of free cash flow, has minimal dilution via stock compensation, is buying back shares aggressively, and is trading at close to a double-digit free cash flow yield. It is well positioned in a growing market that is still underpenetrated; it appears to have a long runway of growth ahead of it. I have spent time with management in person and like their approach; they are motivated, energetic, aligned, and humble. We have started with a small position as I continue my research process, but I am excited by my early findings.

I am studying a number of additional companies as valuations have been reshuffled. As always, I remain focused on the long term, and we are positioned as such. Last quarter, I provided in-depth updates on our top five positions. 2 This quarter, I delve deeper into our portfolio and provide updates on a number of smaller special situation positions.

Special Situations

Special situations have continued to be our best risk-adjusted source of return. Pure event-driven situations have tended to be at most 10-20% of our capital at any given time, as there are only so many of them. Special situations more broadly defined, including spinoffs, value with a catalyst positions, and ideas like the three described below, have been a larger part of the portfolio.

In our fourth quarter 2023 letter, I described two special situations in which we had invested: a delisting and an uplisting. Each of these companies made major strides in the first quarter.

The “Delisting”

We purchased the stock I described as a delisting for approximately $10 3 per share. Frequently, when a company is delisted from a major exchange, certain shareholders become “forced sellers” or, at the very least, motivated sellers. Many large funds can’t hold shares that trade over the counter, for example.

Which exchange or marketplace a company’s shares trade on is not a major concern of ours. Our thesis was that the stock was cheap and that the company would unlock value on its balance sheet over the subsequent years, likely distributing that value to shareholders as it was realized.

The thesis has played out largely as hoped. The company has indeed monetized various assets on its balance sheet; it sold another meaningful asset last quarter and recently distributed the associated cash proceeds. It has now paid out dividends of $17.50 per share since we invested.

Even after these meaningful distributions, I expect that there are still more to come.

The “Uplisting”

The stock I described as an uplisting candidate never did uplist but instead wound up selling itself. The transaction was announced in the first quarter, and we received the majority of our share of the cash proceeds earlier this month (there are still some contingent payments that will hopefully be paid out over the next two years). We earned a very attractive return in this position over a multi-year holding period.

As you can see, patience was required for each of these positions, and it was ultimately rewarded. The delisting did essentially nothing for two years and then went up by about 150% all at once. Similarly, the potential uplisting was flat until it was finally acquired. Unfortunately, these were both small positions. While the companies were each small, there was additional capacity available, so I am to blame for the sizing mistake.

The Demutualization

It is also worth checking in on another special situation—the insurance demutualization—that I initially discussed in our first quarter 2022 letter. In that letter, I described the dynamics of how demutualizations create incentives to set conservative offering prices:

Frequently in an IPO, the prior owners/insiders sell stock, or at the very least are diluted at the IPO price. While there are a number of factors at play in setting an IPO price, the issuer would typically prefer to sell stock for a higher price, not a lower one.

In a demutualization, there are no shareholders prior to the offering (just policyholders with certain governance rights and rights to participate in extraordinary transactions), so these dynamics do not exist. As a result, all conversion-offering investors, including management and insiders (and policyholders), have alignment—everyone wants valuation to be lower rather than higher...

Imagine you (and by “you,” I do not mean you , savvy investor who shares in my love of the hunt for obscure and off-the-beaten-path, highly compelling investment opportunities, but rather “you,” carpenter, taxi driver, student, or clerk at the local dollar store, who just needed some good auto insurance at a fair price) have a policy with a small, local insurance company. One day, you get a proxy statement and dense prospectus in the mail offering the chance to subscribe to securities in the parent company of your insurer. Are you going to request the stock order form and whip out your checkbook? Perhaps not.

This leaves capacity for insiders and others to subscribe at that same “discounted” price.

The offering price that we and insiders paid was $10 per share, as is typical in demutualizations. I believed that this price was a meaningful discount to intrinsic value and more than made up for the expected illiquidity that was to be expected in the shares.

This probably would have been a good investment merely due to the discount we initially received, but management has knocked it out of the park. In 2025, this company generated over $2 of earnings per share and grew tangible book value (which is north of $20 per share) by 13%. The earnings yield on our purchase price is now greater than 20%. Given its strong position in an interesting niche of the specialty insurance market, this business appears to be poised to continue to grow earnings and compound book value over time. $5 per share of earnings power in five years is a possibility.

Small specialty insurance companies typically trade for 1-1.5x book value or 10-15x earnings in the public market and can go for more in buyouts. One could argue this company is worth $25-30 per share today and perhaps will be worth much more in the future. It remains highly illiquid, which doesn't faze me. Small, off the beaten path, and illiquid companies continue to be bountiful sources in our hunt for alpha generation.

In Memoriam: Murray Stahl

A black and white portrait of Murray Stahl. He is a middle-aged man with dark, slightly messy hair and thick-rimmed glasses. He is wearing a white dress shirt and a dark tie. His right hand is raised near his chin, with his fingers slightly curled in a thoughtful gesture. He is looking off to the side with a serious, contemplative expression. The background is dark and out of focus.

Black and white portrait of Murray Stahl, a man with glasses, looking thoughtful, with his hand raised near his face.

The world recently received the incredibly sad news of the passing of Murray Stahl. I’m grateful that my path crossed with Murray’s over the last four years and am filled with sorrow that I will not have the opportunity to continue to learn from, laugh with, and reason about ideas alongside Murray for at least a few more decades.

Murray was erudite, kind, a fantastic story-teller, and hilarious. Murray had integrity; he had principles and stuck to them. He was a man of his word. Just as he was patient with investments, he was patient with people.

Murray did not waver on doing his own research. He was a truly independent thinker and had a unique penchant for thinking long term. He reveled in his anachronisms and idiosyncrasies—can you imagine not having email in this day and age? He knew he wouldn’t be able to convince the world of the wisdom of his ways but never considered reshaping himself to fit the world.

Like me, he was a walker. After dinner in Union Square, Murray wouldn’t dream of catching a taxi for the 25-block trip to Grand Central. No, we would walk it, continuing our conversation about Churchill, the Enlightenment, the history of New York City, his childhood in Brooklyn, the Mafia, or the subject of one of the over 1,000 memoirs and biographies he had read and distilled. That’s when we weren’t discussing investing and his philosophy behind position-sizing, the hallmarks of quality in a business, or specific strategies of individual companies.

Murray was a consummate teacher, and I’m glad I was able to be one of his students, at least for a while.

Murray was one of one, a legend. I will miss him greatly.

Conclusion

Murray Stahl was indeed an incredible teacher, and we should try to remember—and live by—some of his lessons. It isn’t possible to time the market. We should not be universally contrarian but simply be independent thinkers. Think long term and be patient. Concentrate where you have an edge. Read voraciously. Never stop learning. Be kind. Treat others as you’d like to be treated.

But I’ll say this, as most of you probably know, I spent decades trying to convince people to be long-term investors.

Haven’t convinced one person yet.

They all want to trade.

– Murray Stahl
